refined_fr.refined_fr: The NGHE solution shall maintain teleoperation communication packet loss at or below the customer-defined threshold (acceptance criterion: ≤1% under agreed network conditions) to ensure safe and effective remote equipment operation.
refined_fr.rationale: CR-NGHE-009 addresses a critical quality-of-service requirement for teleoperation. Packet loss directly impacts operator situational awareness, control responsiveness, and safety. Refining the FR to explicitly reference the acceptance criterion (≤1%) and network conditions scope clarifies the measurable target and boundary conditions. High safety criticality demands precise definition of what 'communication quality' means operationally.
refined_fr.verification_idea: Conduct packet loss measurement under agreed network conditions (e.g., 4G/5G coverage, latency profile, interference scenarios). Measure end-to-end packet loss across Telemetry (sensor→operator) and Tasking (operator→vehicle) paths. Acceptance: measured packet loss ≤ customer-defined limit across 95th percentile of test scenarios.
sub_frs: FR-NGHE-009.1: The teleoperation system shall transmit operator control commands (Tasking) with packet loss ≤ customer threshold.
FR-NGHE-009.2: The teleoperation system shall transmit vehicle sensor telemetry (Telemetry) with packet loss ≤ customer threshold.
FR-NGHE-009.3: The teleoperation system shall detect and report communication degradation (packet loss exceeding threshold) via SafetyAlert.
FR-NGHE-009.4: The teleoperation system shall implement redundancy or error correction to maintain packet loss within threshold under transient network faults.

candidate_dps: DP-A: Implement adaptive bitrate encoding and packet prioritization in the teleoperation link to reduce payload size and prioritize critical control/safety packets.
DP-B: Deploy dual-path communication (primary + backup network link) with automatic failover to maintain connectivity and reduce effective packet loss.
DP-C: Implement forward error correction (FEC) and packet retransmission with timeout-based recovery to compensate for lost packets.
DP-D: Combine DP-A + DP-B + DP-C: multi-layer resilience strategy (encoding, redundancy, recovery) to meet high safety criticality and customer packet loss threshold.
selected_dp.selected_dp: DP-D: Implement a multi-layer teleoperation resilience strategy combining adaptive bitrate encoding (DP-A), dual-path communication with failover (DP-B), and forward error correction with retransmission (DP-C). This approach addresses high safety criticality by providing defense-in-depth: encoding reduces baseline loss, dual-path provides geographic/provider redundancy, and FEC/retransmission recovers transient losses.

potential_sources: DP-D couples teleoperation module to Network Link interface (dual-path requires multi-network capability).
DP-D couples teleoperation module to Safety Services (SafetyAlert must be triggered on packet loss threshold breach).
DP-D couples teleoperation module to Vehicle (Tasking and Telemetry flows must support FEC overhead and retransmission latency).
coupling_check.notes: Couplings are acceptable and necessary given high safety criticality. Dual-path and FEC are standard industry practice for safety-critical teleoperation (e.g., mining, construction, autonomous vehicles). Coupling to Safety Services is intentional: packet loss detection must trigger safety alerts. Coupling to Vehicle is manageable via interface versioning (Tasking/Telemetry can accommodate FEC headers).

assumptions_risks: ASSUMPTION: Customer-defined packet loss threshold is ≤1% and is achievable under agreed network conditions with multi-layer resilience.
ASSUMPTION: Dual-path network infrastructure (two independent carriers or network types) is available at the worksite.
RISK: FEC overhead increases bandwidth consumption; if network bandwidth is severely constrained, FEC may paradoxically increase effective packet loss due to congestion.
RISK: Failover latency (switching from primary to backup path) may cause transient control loss; operator training and safety procedures must account for this.
RISK: Retransmission timeout tuning is critical; too short causes unnecessary retransmits, too long causes unacceptable control lag.
selected_dp.recommended_revision: Refine DP-D to explicitly specify: (1) FEC scheme (e.g., Reed-Solomon, Turbo codes) and overhead budget, (2) failover detection and switching time (e.g., <500ms), (3) retransmission timeout strategy (e.g., adaptive based on RTT), (4) packet prioritization rules (safety-critical packets get priority over telemetry). Add a sub-DP for monitoring and alerting: 'DP-D.5: Implement real-time packet loss monitoring and automatic SafetyAlert trigger when loss exceeds threshold for >N consecutive packets.'

im_boundary.name: Teleoperation
im_boundary.scope: The Teleoperation module owns the end-to-end teleoperation communication pipeline: encoding/decoding, dual-path routing, FEC/retransmission logic, packet loss monitoring, and SafetyAlert triggering. Interfaces with Vehicle (Tasking/Telemetry), Network Link (dual-path connectivity), and Safety Services (SafetyAlert output).

external_interfaces.name: Tasking
external_interfaces.details: Operator control commands flow into Teleoperation from the operator console (or tele-operator workstation). Teleoperation encodes, applies FEC, and transmits via dual-path Network Link to Vehicle.

external_interfaces.name: Telemetry
external_interfaces.details: Vehicle sensor data flows from Vehicle into Teleoperation via dual-path Network Link. Teleoperation decodes, applies FEC recovery, and outputs to operator console. Packet loss on this path is monitored.

external_interfaces.name: Network Link
external_interfaces.details: Teleoperation uses Network Link as the transport layer for dual-path communication. Teleoperation manages failover logic and monitors link quality (packet loss, latency, jitter) on both primary and backup paths.

external_interfaces.name: SafetyAlert
external_interfaces.details: Teleoperation triggers SafetyAlert when packet loss exceeds customer threshold or when failover occurs. Safety Services consumes this alert to initiate safety procedures (e.g., operator warning, equipment shutdown).

module_containment_scores.notes: Teleoperation is the natural owner of packet loss resilience logic. All encoding, FEC, dual-path routing, and monitoring are core teleoperation concerns. Minimal diffusion: only SafetyAlert output and Network Link dependency are external.

Vehicle should not own teleoperation resilience; it is a consumer of Tasking and producer of Telemetry. Placing FEC/dual-path logic in Vehicle would scatter teleoperation concerns across two modules and violate separation of concerns.
Safety Services consumes SafetyAlert but should not own packet loss detection or FEC logic. Safety Services is a consumer of alerts, not a producer of teleoperation resilience.

NGHE Solution is the system-level container but is too coarse-grained to own teleoperation packet loss logic. Assigning to NGHE Solution would defer the architectural decision and scatter implementation across sub-modules.

assigned_im.containment_hypothesis: Teleoperation module is the optimal owner of DP-D (multi-layer resilience). It has the highest containment score (95) and lowest diffusion (5). All teleoperation-specific concerns (encoding, FEC, dual-path routing, packet loss monitoring) are naturally contained within Teleoperation. External interfaces (Tasking, Telemetry, Network Link, SafetyAlert) are well-defined and minimal.
containment_risks: RISK: Teleoperation module may become complex if FEC, dual-path routing, and monitoring are all implemented in a single module. Mitigation: decompose Teleoperation into sub-modules (e.g., Encoding, Routing, Monitoring) at the next level.

RISK: Network Link interface must support dual-path abstraction (primary + backup). If Network Link is not designed for multi-path, Teleoperation will need to manage raw network sockets, increasing coupling. Mitigation: ensure Network Link interface provides dual-path primitives.

RISK: SafetyAlert interface must be real-time and reliable. If Safety Services is slow to consume SafetyAlert, Teleoperation may need to implement local safety logic (e.g., command timeout). Mitigation: define SafetyAlert latency SLA (e.g., <100ms).
assigned_im.recommended_revision: Confirm that Network Link interface supports dual-path abstraction (e.g., 'select_primary_path()', 'select_backup_path()', 'get_link_quality(path_id)'). If not, revise Network Link interface to expose multi-path primitives. Confirm SafetyAlert interface supports real-time, reliable delivery (e.g., via message queue or event bus). If not, revise SafetyAlert interface or add a local timeout mechanism in Teleoperation.

fr_dp_gate.reason: DP-D (multi-layer resilience) directly addresses FR-NGHE-009 (packet loss ≤ customer threshold). The combination of adaptive encoding, dual-path failover, and FEC/retransmission is a proven approach for safety-critical teleoperation. DP-D is implementable and testable. No decoupling or missing logic detected.

diffusion_risks: Teleoperation module will depend on Network Link for dual-path primitives. If Network Link is not mature, Teleoperation may need to implement low-level socket management, increasing complexity.
Teleoperation module will depend on Safety Services for SafetyAlert consumption. If Safety Services is slow or unreliable, Teleoperation may need local safety fallbacks.
dp_im_gate.containment_notes: Teleoperation is the clear owner of DP-D. Containment score is 95/100. Diffusion is minimal (only external interfaces to Network Link and Safety Services). No alternative module is suitable. Ownership is unambiguous.

fr_im_gate.verification_feasibility: Verification of FR-NGHE-009 is feasible: conduct packet loss measurement under agreed network conditions (4G/5G, latency profile, interference). Measure end-to-end loss on Tasking and Telemetry paths. Acceptance: measured loss ≤ customer threshold (e.g., ≤1%) across 95th percentile of test scenarios. Teleoperation module can be tested in isolation via network simulation (e.g., tc (traffic control) on Linux, Clumsy on Windows) to inject packet loss and verify FEC/failover behavior.
fr_im_gate.allocation_clarity: Allocation is clear: Teleoperation owns packet loss resilience. Sub-FRs (FR-NGHE-009.1 through 009.4) are allocated to Teleoperation. No ambiguity or overlap with other modules.

recommended_revisions: Refine Network Link interface specification to include dual-path primitives (e.g., 'select_path(primary|backup)', 'get_link_quality(path_id)', 'failover_event()').
Refine SafetyAlert interface specification to include latency SLA (e.g., <100ms) and reliability guarantee (e.g., at-least-once delivery).
Add a sub-DP for Teleoperation: 'DP-D.5: Implement real-time packet loss monitoring and automatic SafetyAlert trigger when loss exceeds threshold for >N consecutive packets (e.g., N=10).'
Add a verification plan: 'Conduct packet loss injection testing using network simulation tools (tc, Clumsy) to verify FEC recovery and failover behavior under various loss rates (0.1%, 1%, 5%, 10%).'

Add a risk mitigation plan: 'If Network Link does not support dual-path primitives, implement a Network Link abstraction layer in Teleoperation to manage raw sockets. If Safety Services is slow, implement a local timeout mechanism in Teleoperation to trigger emergency stop if SafetyAlert is not consumed within SLA.'

decision.justification: FR-NGHE-009 is not atomic; it decomposes into four sub-FRs (FR-NGHE-009.1 through 009.4) addressing distinct concerns: Tasking packet loss, Telemetry packet loss, degradation detection, and redundancy/recovery. DP-D is sound and well-justified, but its implementation requires further decomposition of Teleoperation module into sub-modules (e.g., Encoding, Routing, Monitoring, Recovery). The next level of decomposition will refine how DP-D is realized within Teleoperation and allocate sub-FRs to sub-modules. Gate review passes; no revisions to FR, DP, or IM are required at this level. Proceed to decompose sub-FRs and Teleoperation module.
next_level_sub_frs: FR-NGHE-009.1: The teleoperation system shall transmit operator control commands (Tasking) with packet loss ≤ customer threshold under agreed network conditions.
FR-NGHE-009.2: The teleoperation system shall transmit vehicle sensor telemetry (Telemetry) with packet loss ≤ customer threshold under agreed network conditions.
FR-NGHE-009.3: The teleoperation system shall detect and report communication degradation (packet loss exceeding threshold) via SafetyAlert within <100ms of detection.

FR-NGHE-009.4: The teleoperation system shall implement redundancy or error correction to maintain packet loss within threshold under transient network faults (e.g., single-path loss >5% for >1 second).

assumptions: Customer-defined packet loss threshold is ≤1% and is achievable under agreed network conditions with multi-layer resilience.
Dual-path network infrastructure (two independent carriers or network types) is available at the worksite.
Network Link interface can be extended to support dual-path primitives (e.g., 'select_path()', 'get_link_quality()').
Safety Services can consume SafetyAlert with latency <100ms and reliability guarantee (at-least-once delivery).
Operator training and safety procedures account for failover latency (e.g., <500ms) and transient control loss.
FEC scheme (e.g., Reed-Solomon) and overhead budget are acceptable within bandwidth constraints.
open_risks: RISK: FEC overhead may increase bandwidth consumption; if network bandwidth is severely constrained, FEC may paradoxically increase effective packet loss due to congestion. Mitigation: conduct bandwidth analysis and FEC overhead tuning during detailed design.
RISK: Failover latency may cause transient control loss. Mitigation: define failover SLA (e.g., <500ms) and validate via testing.
RISK: Retransmission timeout tuning is critical. Mitigation: implement adaptive timeout based on RTT and validate via network simulation.
RISK: Network Link interface may not support dual-path primitives. Mitigation: extend Network Link interface or implement abstraction layer in Teleoperation.
RISK: Safety Services may be slow to consume SafetyAlert. Mitigation: define SafetyAlert latency SLA and implement local timeout mechanism in Teleoperation if needed.
evidence_needed: Network Link interface specification (confirm dual-path support or plan for extension).
SafetyAlert interface specification (confirm latency SLA and reliability guarantee).
Bandwidth analysis: estimate FEC overhead and confirm it fits within network capacity.
Failover latency measurement: validate that failover time is <500ms under various network conditions.
Packet loss injection testing: verify FEC recovery and failover behavior under 0.1%, 1%, 5%, 10% loss rates.
Operator training plan: confirm that operators understand failover behavior and safety procedures.
Customer acceptance criteria: confirm that ≤1% packet loss is the agreed threshold and that test scenarios are representative of actual worksite conditions.
